content.problemStatement:
Shubman Gill is playing an international match.

He played a total of NNN balls, where, in the ithi^{th}ith ball, he scored AiA_{i}Ai​ runs.

The strike rate of a player is calculated as : no. of runsno. of balls played×100\frac{\text{no. of runs}}{\text{no. of balls played}}\times 100no. of balls playedno. of runs​×100.

Preet, a math enthusiast, is currently watching the match. Help him find the number of times, Shubman's strike rate became **exactly** 100100100.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the total number of balls played by Gill.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers where AiA_{i}Ai​ denotes runs scored on ithi^{th}ith ball.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the total number of times the strike rate of Gill became 100100100.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of balls.
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN, where Ai is the runs scored on the i‑th ball.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of prefixes (1 ≤ i ≤ N) for which the strike rate is exactly 100, i.e., the total runs after i balls equals i.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: Prefix sums are [1,1,3,6]; positions 1 and 3 satisfy sum=i → 2 times.
Test case 2: No prefix has sum equal to its length → 0.
Test case 3: Prefix sums are [1,2,3]; all three positions satisfy → 3.
Only one ball with 0 runs. Sum=0 ≠ 1, so strike rate is never 100.
Prefix sums: [1,3,6,6,6,6]. Positions 1 and 6 have sum equal to index.

solution.approach:
The strike rate becomes 100 exactly when total runs = number of balls played. While scanning the balls we keep a running sum of runs. For each position i (1‑based) we compare the current sum with i; if they are equal we increment the answer. This is a single pass O(N) algorithm per test case.

Algorithm:
1. Read T.
2. Loop over test cases:
   a. Read N.
   b. Initialise sum = 0, ans = 0.
   c. For i from 1 to N:
        - read Ai,
        - sum += Ai,
        - if sum == i then ans++.
   d. Output ans.
The algorithm uses O(1) extra memory and runs in O(total N) time, which satisfies the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of balls across all test cases) ≤ O(10^6)
